Annotation: Diuels appeare not in Gods sight, but sometime in presence of Angels which represent God. S. Athan. q 2. ad Antiochum.
Satan also was present amongst them.
God doth not directly fend euils, but permitteth the hand to happen to his seruants.

Simple, right, and fearing God.) Moyfes, Samuel, Esdras, and other Prophets writ there owne actes among others, also their owne prayses, speaking of themselues in the third person, and so did holie Iob, humbly, truly, and simply, without vayne glorie, or arrogancie, as S. Gregorie noteth, faying: Blessed Iob, inspired with the Holie Ghost, might wel write his owne actes, which were the guifts of God being the principal cause efficient of al good mens workes, and men the secondarie cause of the same.

Offered holocausts for euerie one.] Albeit the vertue of Sacrifice as wel of Holocaust in the old Testament, as especially of Christs Sacrifice in the New, is of infinit value in it-selfe, yet the application thereof to particular persons and purposes is limited, and therefore holie Iob offered not only once for al his children, but manie times, feuerally for euerie one. Wherof see Cardinal Athan. li. 2. de Eucharist. Sacrific. c. 35.
A man of plaine & true dealing mixed with meeknes without al guile is called a simple right man. S. Greg. li. c. Moral. c. 2. &c.
His wife perswading him to desperation & blasphemie, signified carnal cogitations, which corrupt the soule inwardly, as afflictions doe assault the flesh outwardly. S. Greg. li. 3. c. 4.
Seuen dayes together euerie day & night some good part.

Iobs three freinds.] For better intelligence of these conflicts betweene holie Iob and his freinds, it may here be obserued, that they were indeed his freinds, as the text simply calleth them: that they belieued rightly in God almightie, and were not idolaters: that they came of freinuly good affection to comfort him, though they fell into words of reprouing him (as S. Gregorie teacheth:) li. 3. 6. they alleadged also many excellent diuine sentences very truly, which therfore 24. Iobreproued not. But they erred in rations against Iob: and that of ignorance rather then of set malice, concluding that Iob was guiltie of some enormous sinnes, because they saw him so grieuously punished, and heard him complaine thereof; his owne conscience knowen to him and hid to them (wherot they raihly iudged, testifying that he was innocent, in respect of so great crimes. And prefigured Heretikes, in this their particular errour, though they were not heretikes, being not obstinate after that the truth was sufficiently reueiled vnto them, yet they prefigured heretikes, endeauouring by one truth to destroy an other, and by arrogating knowledge which they had not: promising also new things and vnheard of; rathers to draw others to admire them, then to edification. Li. 5. c. 18.

After fo long silence, at last Iob expresseth his grief before his freinds, to be so great, that in respect thereof he mildly curseth al euil things, as not made by God, but turned to mans life, being the effects of sinne, euen from his conception and birth: wishing that whatsoever concurred to his longer afflictions in this life & hindred his more speedy deliuerie from dangers & c. a famiries had nor been: for so he had been sooner arred. as Gods goodnes should dispose of him. Al which was a lawful desire & no sinne at al. See S. Vincentda in c. 2. Iob.
No innocent euer perished eternally: but innocent Abel was slaine temporally, & innumerable others suffer calamities for their greater merite.
Heretikes pretend such obfcure visions more to get credit then to edifie others. S.Greg. l,5.c.18.
Tob easely granteth that man may not compare nor contend with God, ch.9.v.1. Yet men may be innocent & free from grievous sinnes.

Turne to some of the Saints. Eliphaz prouoking Iob to produce some of his opinion, or to seeke the helpe and patronage of some Saint in his cause, plainly sheweth the common faith and practise of inuocating Saints in that time. Els it had been a friuolous speach, which is not to be imputed to a sensible wise man as he was. For it appeareth by the drift of his reasoning, that he supposed some of Gods special seruants would maintaine a good cause, but that Iobs cause was such as neither God, nor holie Angel, nor good man would defend, and therfore boldly prouoked him to this trial, presuming that he should finde no such patron. Neither did he wil Iob in these words to cal vpon God only, for he could not erre so grosly, as to cal God some of the Saints: but must meane some other holie person. And it is cleer by the Septuaginta Interpreters, that Eliphaz willed Iob to inuocate the Angels, saying: Inuecate if anie wil answer thee, or if thou canst behold anie of the holie Angels. S. Gregorie expoundeth it to the same sense, that Saints li. 5. c. were to be inuocated in a good cause, but that Eliphaz here dispising and deriding holie Iob, sayd to him: Thou canst not find Saints thy helpers in affliction, whom thou wouldst not haue thy fellowes in prosperitie.
This Prouerb importeth that a man must not thinke to passe his life without trauel but must get his bread with sweate of his braves, or suffer other calamities.
Gods goodnes deliuereth his seruants the space of this laborious life.
And most especially in the houre of death. S.Greg. li. 6. c. 18.
A man of sincere conscience confesseth the sinnes wherof he is guiltie, yet a knowledgeth no alwhere with others may vniastly charge him.

As the sand of the sea. Scarce anie figure is more common in holie Scripture then Hyperbole: wherby our vnderstanding is drawne to conceiue the greatnes of things that otherwise surpasse vulgar capacitie. So Iob signifieth here that his calamitie being weighed with his sinnes in a balance, would appeare heauier, not precisely in proportion of the number of the sands in the sea, out exceeding much in true comparison. Of which superaboundance of paines patiently suffered by holie Iob, and other Saints, more then their sinnes deserued, especially of our B. Sauiour, who could not sinne, and of our B. Ladie, who neuer sinned, remayneth an infinitie treasure of satisfactorie workes, appliable by the supreme stuard of Gods Church, Christs Vicar in earth, for satisfaction of others which haue neede, and are in the communion of Saints, and performe the conditions appointed. And so this high stuard may in due manner by his authoritie, giue limited, or plenarie pardons to penitents, of the paines which otherwise they should suffer in this life, or in purgatorie, for their sinnes first remitted. Other Bishops can also giue or dispence so much as the Supreme Bishop allotteth to their power: al to the edification and necessitie of Gods seruants, as dispencers, not dissipatours of so holie a treasure.
Sand ie was without water.

Iobs aduersaries presumed to teach him, but because they erred in their applying of true assertions in his case, which himselfe vnderstood and not they, he accounted not their discourses for good doctrine.

A souldier must be alwayes readie to endure trauel, to be promptly obedient, content to be beaten by his superiour without al resistance, vpon paine of his life; he is alwayes subiect to cares, and to danger of death, & therfore must euer be readie to dye.

I haue despayred. I thal now liue yo ionger: Spare me, for my daies are nothing. What is man that thou magnifiest him? or why settest thou thy hart toward him? Thou doest visite him early, and sodainly thou prouest him. How long doest thou not spare me, nor suffer me that I swallow my spitle? I haue sinned, what shall I doe to thee, o keeper of men: why hast thou sette me contrarie to thee, and I am become burdenous to my self? Why doest thou not take away my sinne,
The second conflict. This second disputer charged Job to be obstinate; who indeed was constant in a true settled judgement.

Even thus Heretikes imagine catholikes to be ignorant, & therefore fit their mouths & books with things that are not denied, & yet inferre much falshood sophisticaly, applying one truth against another, being themselves ignorant how to reconcile difficulties.
Job here granteth that which was truly said by his adversarie, and sheweth how he did wrong aptly true doctrine against him, and so stil defendeth his owne innocencie and just quarrel.
Angels come to me, I shal not see him: if he depart, I shal not vaderstand.
This is an affured true assertion, that God afflicteth both good & evil in this life.
He crieth on mans part, and hope in God, doe wel consist together. So both presumption and desperation are avoided.
Holie Job knowing it to be vnpossible that God calumniateth. As a man, inquireth what is the cause why his goodnes afflicteth the iust.
Repentance is alwayes necessarie, but most especially at the houre of death.
Sophar might haue applied the vice of much speaking to himselfe & his fellowes, alleadging manie things which proued not their opinion, wheras Iobs allegations proued directly that which he affirmed.
Iobs owne conscience affirmed the contrarie, ch. 6, v. 3.
He could not iustly confesse inquirie which he had not committed.

God suffereth his simple true-meaning seruants to be scorned for the time, but afterward the wicked shal be forced to concede, that those whom they decided are worthily in honour before God. S. ep. 5, v. 3.
Craftie plotters of deuises at last by Gods iust punishment commit so great absurdities, that the meanest may see their follie.
Iobs state of sinne or innocencie was best knowen to God,next to his owne conscience,not at all to his aduersaries,that professed to iudge of sinnes,that pre fissed to iudge thereof.
He denieth that he is guiltie in conscience,but defereth to know of God;if he haue anie hid sinnes which himselfe knoweth not.

Al children taking their substance from the mother, and hauing only a temporal life continually tending to death, can not but be subiect to manie miseries: For time it-selfe though it may seeme long, is a limited thing, and is very short, yea hath no proportion in respect of eternitie,which is infinite: yet God hath care to bring this weake creature to life-eternal.
The prementres after death are not afflicted with the tare of their children as they be in this life.
Eliphaz not able to answere Iobs solide reasons,raileth against him, as he was rious to God, or taught others to cast away feare.
It is a very rare priuiledge to be without spot.
But spot of veniall sinnes may be in a iust man.
Al these miseries are incident to the wicked,but are falsly applied to holy Iob, who indeed was iust.
True and freindlie comforters ought to heare the afflicted with patience, & not vnmercifully charge him with crimes, which they neither know, nor his conscience is guiltie of.
A great affliction, when one ful of paine & distresse is also forced to defend his owne innocencie against ca. lumniators.
As the aduersaries stil obiect great iniquitie to him, so he yealdeth them the fame true answere.
Not hauing committed such sinnes as he was charged withal,in this double bitternes of corporal paine & calumniation,his eye was stil vpon God,expecting to be deliuered.
This text sheweth euidently that there was a place of rest called hel.

Baldad perceiuing Iob to speake confidently as the common doctrine of manie, opposeth himselfe neuerthe les against him and al that thinke as he speaketh as to manie in the plural number, wil ye speake, & of himselfe & his felowes: are we reputed. &c. So holie Iob a figure of the Church defended the cause, his aduersaries a figure of heretikes speaking some truth mixed false things therwith. S.Greg.li. 14. c.t.

Blessed Iob faith S. Gregorie li. 14. c. 16. 17. looking sincerely on his owne life, saw that his affliction was greater then his finnes deserued, & in that respect was not equall: yet it was iust, for God being iust giueth a iust reward as crowned of iustice as S. Paul speaketh of himselfe (2. Tim. 4.)
Al refused made holie Iob in his affliction, euen those whom he had loued most who therfore ought most to haue loued him againe.
An expresse profession of his faith of the Resurrection.
We shal rise againe, not as one tree riseth in place of an other, but the selfe same persons, changed in qualities, not in substance.

Al mans life is short & as a moment in comparison of eternitie; but it is not alwayes true that the wicked are shortly punished in re spect of this life, as this disputer applieth it to proue Iob to be impious.

Therefore Iob answereth in the next chap. v. 13. That ordinarily the wicked lead their dayes in their whole life in wealth & (then) in a mon‡ goe downe to hel. So the rest of these mens assertions are for most part true in some sense, but il applied.
Though he disputed with three men, yet it was concerning diuine things not humane, but of Gods prouidence, and iustice, of the resurrection, of eternall life, and punishment.

The fame word is in Hebrew, Greek, & Latin, for hel, as in the 7. and 17. chap. & other places, which sheweth that besides hel of the damned the resting place also of holie fathers in the old Testament was called by the general name of hel;
Who sayd to God: Depart from vs.

Indeed when a iust man hath donne his duties, he is vnprofitable to God: but he is profitable to himselfe, which greatly pleaseth God, who desireth mans good, & it redoundeth to Gods glorie that he hath such seruants.
After imitation of false crimes, this disputer chargeth holie Iob also with heathenish errour of the Egyptians, that God hath no prouidence of men in this world. Aristotel. li. de mundo, textu. 84.
So some heretikes in their phrensie accuse Catholikes of condemned heresies.
Whiles he expected fome comfort of his freinds, they stil afflicted him more & more, charging him with false crimes & fo aggrauating his grief both of bodie and mind.
Therefore he appeareth to Gods iudgement feare for sentence.
Gods seruants know that he wil punish wickednes, but know not when the wicked presume, that he wil neuer punish them.
Heretikes do long and teaching against their owne knowledge, are afterward strucken with blindnes, that they can not see the truth. S. Greg. li. 16. c. 25
Sinners running into both extremes of defect and excesse, are like wise punished with contrarie tormentes.
Man by these precious & rare creatures, men ought to consider the Creatour, & so not set their rest in them, but in him, which is true wisdome.
Iob answered before, ch. 4. yet blind and obstinate disputers stil repeate the same objections.
Can man be iustified compared with God, or the borne of a woman appeare cleane?
Iob sheweth that neither God needeth mans helpe: Neither is Baldads prudence able to help, if there were neede.
Giants were not able to wade in Noes floud, but were drowned with the rest.
Not only great things before recited, but also the very least are made by God, and depend vpon his prouidcnce.
God would not as yet haue Iobs cause iudged, but referred the sentence, for his greater trial in patience.
It were a lie to acknowledge such sinnes as he had not committed.
Some part of Gods iudgement falleth on the wicked in this life, but especially at their death. Psal. 75.
By these more precious & rare creatures, men ought to consider the Creatour, & so not set their rest in them, but in him, which is true wisdome.
True wisdome is not in naturall things, but in supernaturall vertues.
When man hath considered, and prepared, and searched it, Behold the feare of our Lord, that is wisedom: and to depart from euil, vnderstanding.
Parables are not only similitudes of things, but also pithie and profound sentences, such as Iob, Salomon, and other wiseit men vttered.
This parti- cle as; impor- teeth not here a similitude, but rather that he was a very King or su- preme Prince, as hauing su- preme autho- ritie.
Men scarce fit to haue care of dogs deri- ded Iob in his affliction:so he was contemned of the most contemptible.
Our Sauiour also suffered this reproch, Mar.25.
Death is a comfort to a iust man in tri- bulation.
Like to such beasts as seeke solitary places to lament in.

Whereas here is a continuall warre betweene a chast mind and rebellious flesh, holy Iob made this con- dicion of truce betweene these enemies: that his eye should neuer giue oc- casion to car- nal concupis- cence.
By this de- mand he pro- uoked his ad- uersaries to produce what they could to conuince him of idolatrie or denying God, wherwith they indirectly charged him.
Iob dispu- ted no more with his freinds but afterward submitted him- self to God, ac- knowledging some vnadui- sed speach.
The words of Iob are ended.
The ninth conflict. This yong man wittie & learned, but proud withal, was a figure of the bore and arrogans dif- puters, who wil seeme to know more then their elders. S. Greg.l.23.c.1.
A notorious arrogancie to hold himselfe wiser then any man, either of his owne sect, or of his ad- uersaries.

Those that neither credite Catholike Doctours, nor yet relie vpon their owne el- ders, but euery one vpon his owne priuate spirit, denie that to per- teyne to them which is spo- ken to others in the same er- rours. S.Greg.l. 23 c. 8.
Arrogant men imagine their owne conceits and vterance to be more merue- lous then other mens, not knowing how foolish their owne pride is. S. Greg. li. 23. 6.
Iob said no that God iudged wrongfully or wrongfully iudged, but God hath taken away my iudgement, cha. 27. to that is, deferred to indga my cause.
Neither did Iob say this c. v.v. 11.) but Eliu wrested his meaning, that he might haue something to reprehend. S.Greg. li. 14. c. 15.

Eliu applieth this to Iob, as though he had made false shew of vertue which he had not, and that God had suffered him hitherto to reigne, or rather to tyrannize for iust punishment of his peoples sinnes. But God at last declared that Iob was not such a one, ch. 42. v.7.

Eliu insisteth much in this calumniation: for Iob neuer said, that he was iust compared with God, nor iuster then God. But that his affliction was greater then his sinne. cha v. v. 3. c. 23. v. 7. &c.
These are strong sentences (faith S. Gregorie) but they agree not (or be euil applied) to the blessed person of Iob. li.16.
No oratour is so vaine but he promiseth al truth, & speaketh some to get creditewith his auditorie.
If Kings reigne wel their praise remaineth for euer. v. 11.
The night is drawne long, when tentations are not speedily resisted.

Aboue al other Law-giuers God is most able to punish transgressours, but most willing & most able to reward the obseruers. S. Gregorie expoundeth this to be a prophecie of Christ our singular Law-giuer. 27. c. 1.
worse & worse cogitations succeed in place of the first. S. Greg. l. 26. c. 38.
Christ wil giue the glorious light of heauen, which now is hidden to men that lost terrestrial paradise. ibidem. c. 12.
Consideration of heauenly rewards mentioned in the end of the former chapter, comforteth the afflicted: but thunder and other meteors being figures of Gods iudgement, strike the hart with terrour.
North wind, or north-pole.
God directeth the clouds in the ayre, as a master mariner gouerneth a ship.
Man not able to praise God sufficiently, prayfeth him with feare.
Iob conuinced the former three with found answers this last and most arrogant with silence.
The third part. The earth and the last dispute. God discusseth the controuerfie, and giueth sentence for Iob.
Onlie the Creatour hath absolute and perfect knowledge of al creatures.
In some Editions the 39.chap beginneth here.
Wild goats.
Wilde asses.
Vnicornes.
Ostriches.
Horsses are of singular great courago
Haukes; wherof Aristotel faith there be ten kinds: Plinie sixteen.
Eagles, at most strong-fight.

Though Iob had truly auouched that his sinnes deserued not so great afflictions, yet he ought to haue acknowledged that God afflicted him iustly, for some other cause knowne to God, but vnknowen to him: which he not confessing seemed to make Gods iudgement vniust or of none effect. S. Greg. l. 33. c. 5.

An Elephant the greatest of al beasts, of long life, strong, meeke, temperate, chast, ouercome by the Vnicorne, or Dragon, or taken by the nose & led away. How much more doth Gods prouidence giue man power to ouercome the diuel?
An huge great fish, perhaps the whale, exceeding mans power to be managed, yet is subiect as also the diuel signified therby, to Gods power, & prouidence.
God ruleth al his creatures, not with crueltie as a tyrant, but with iustice, ease, & power.
God at last destroyeth him whom man can not ouercome.
Angels with reuerent feare doe honour Gods power.
And valient mariners and other soldiers are terrified when they see this so huge a fish. Mystically, Gods preachares & perfected seruants shal naturally feare the terrour of Gods iudgement.
And the diuel reigneth ouer proud men. S. Greg. ii. 34. 6. 4. & 77.
Iob here simply acknowledged his errour in speaking so much in defence of his owne innocencie, & so litle of Gods prouidence in afflicting him, for his more merite, & Gods more glorie.
Before he defended a truth against his opposite freinds, now with more resignation he contented himselfe with his affliction.
God by his sentence condemned the errour of Iobs freinds, and iustified his assertion.
Prayers of holy men or Saints derogate not from Christ.
Iob did penaunce both for himselfe and others.
In that he had al other things double, and children in the same number as before, it is a signe that the former perished not, but died in good state.

VVifdom.) As wel in thefe Sapiential books,as in other holie Scriptures, and sacred writers, the word wifdom hath three fignifications. Sometimes it importeth the Diuine Attribute called Gods wifdom; fometimes fupernatural wifdom giuen to men by the Holie Ghoft; and fometimes it fignifieth mere humane wifdom, gotten by the natural light of reafon and mans induftrie. The firft, as likewife other Diuine Attributes, Gods Power, Goodnes, Iuftic:, Truth, Mercie, and the like, are not qualities, or other accidents in God, as the fame termes fignifie in creatures, but his Diuine Subftance and Effence, whofe diuers Excellencies are called by fuch names as mans capacitie can better conceiue: and fo Gods wifdom is God himfelfe: and is appropriated to the fecond Perfon of the bleffed Trinitie, as Power is approprieted to God the Father, and Goodnes ro the Holie Ghoft.

Because I called and you refused.) God voutfaffeth foure benefits of grace to euerie man, al neceffarie and fufficient for his faluation: 1. He calleth al by preaching, or good infpiration. 2. He offereth help. 3. He inftructeth the ignorant what is good, that they may choofe it if they wil. 4. And reprehendeth euil, that they may fhun it. They therfore that neglect this manifold grace in this life, fhal without al remedie be damned, being too late to repent in the other world. For then they fhal crie and not be heard.
But he that heareth me.) Contrariwise thofe that accept Gods grace, and cooperate therewith, fhal haue eternal reft and ioy.

VVho leaue the right vvay.) Generally this defcription of wicked men, agreeth to al that commit and perift in mortal finne; but moft efpecially fheweth the properties of heretikes: who forfake the direct, ancient, beaten, knowne way of the Catholike Church, and teach new obfcure doctrines, not heard of, or not approued in forefathers time. Secondly they glorie in their owne deuifes, and reioyce in moft wicked things. Thirdly Herefie tempereth her words to pleafe the itching eares of her auditorie. Fourthly thofe that doe enter into errour of herefie, fhal not returne; that is, very hardly and rarely returne into the right way of life.
For whom our Lord loueth he chafticeth: and as a father in the fonne he pleafeth himfelf.
The firft part of wifdom is to defire it. For nothing hindereth from being iuft but that iuftice is not defired. S. Aug. in Pfal. 118.v.20.
As the hart is the principal part of the bodie, fo the wil is the chiefeft power of the foule: from which good or euil proceedeth.
By woman is generally vnderftood concupifcence of what finne foeuer.

The wifman doth not abfolutely difwade from al manner of furetifhip, but from rafh, or vnaduifedly anfwering for others. And efpecially exhorteth to vfe al diligence in performing, or caufing others to performe that which is promifed or couenanted.

Euerie one that finneth witingly and of malice, refufing to obey God, imploueth his mouth, eies, feete, hands, and al parts with a wicked hart and intention to peruerte others: moft proper to heretikes, apoftates from the faith.
The former fix are al damnable, but this feuenth is moft deteftable, becaufe it is oppofite to the chief vertue charitie, it breaketh vnitie, & is the proper finne of the diuel.
Aboccafios of finne, efpecially probable, are to be fhunned.
Theft is alfo mortal finne againft the feuenth commandment, but not fo great as adulterie.

Sinners after confent giuen to temptations are as inconfiderate of their owne ftate, and of their peril and ruine, as an oxe when he is ledde to the fhamles, or a bird allured with a bate, that flyeth into the fnare or net.
True wifdom directing to good life & fo to eternal faluation, is only found in the vifible Church ftanding vpon a mountaine, not hidde in corners or obfcure places.
Thefe fingular praifes perteine to the increated wifdom, God himfelf, from whom wifdom giuen to men by the Holie Ghoft.
God much preferreth man before al other corporal creatures.

VVifdom hath built herfelf a houfe.) According to the literal fenfe, wherin the myftical is grounded (both intended by the Holie Ghoft) Wifdome which is God himfelf, Creatour & Conferuer of al things, whofe fpecial good pleafure and delight is to be with men, built his houfe the Church, firft in the Patriarchs, Priefts, Prophets, and his other faithful feruants in the old Teftament. And in the Allegorical fenfe, the fame diuine increated Wifdome, the fecond Perfon in the B. Trinitie, the Diuine WORD coeternal to the Father, built himfelf a houfe, his humane bodie in the Virgins womb, and therunto adioyncd the members, his myftical bodie the Church, immolated victims of Martyrs, prepared the Table in breade and wine, where alfo appeareth his Priefthood; according to the Order of Melchifedec. As S. Auguftin expoundeth this paffage, li.17, c.20. de ciuit.
Where is no hope of amendment prudence directeth vs not to admonifh, nor rebuke finners, left without anie fruite we procure emnitie; charitie alfo requireth rather to expect better opportunity.

A way which feemeth iuft.) If anie Iewes, Turkes, or Heretikes lead a moral good life in this world, it feemeth both to themfelues, and to other rude people, that they are in a right way of faluation, but their errour in faith leadeth them to eternal damnation.

Health of the hart.) As foundnes of the hart conferneth the reft of the fame bodie in life, fo a pure intention often excufeth from mortal finne, as in errours committed of probable, nor of groffe, nor affected ignorance. But fecret enuie in the hart infecteth and putrifieth mans actions, and deftroyeth the worke that feemed good.

It perteineth to man to prepare the hart.) For the better vnderftanding of this and other hard places of Holie Scripture, this general rule, euer approued by al Chriftians, is moft neceffarie: That al holie Scripture is true and certaine, as being al indited by the Holie Ghoft, the Spirite of truth. And fo S. Auguftin, and other Fathers teach, that the wifeman here affirmeth not, that man of himfelf can prepare his hart, or begin a good worke, but that it perteineth to man to prepare his hart, prefuppofing helpe of Gods grace, and hauing fo begun, God alfo gouerneth the tongue, and by more grace directeth it to fpeake thofe things wel, which the hart purpofed.

Seuentimes fhal the iuft fal.) A iuft man, that is to fay, Gods true feruant, free from mortal finne, is fubiect during this life to manie tentations, imperfections, and may often fal into venial finnes, and not lofe iuftice, nor the true title of a iuft man, nor become the diuels feruant, nor Gods enemie: but therby through Gods grace helping his weaknes, he rifeth againe from venial finnes, ftil perferuering in Gods fauour.

The words of the Gatherer.) Some Interpreters take thefe foure Hebrew words, Agur, Iache, Ithiel, and Vcal, conteined in this firft verfe, to be proper names of men. But the old Interpreter, whom S. Ierom approueth and foloweth, tranflated the fame words as noones appellatiues. And therefore we thinke it more probable, with S. Beda, and the common opinion, that there was no other auctor of anie part of this booke, befides King Salomon.

A bond-woman when fhe fhal be heyre.) Of al things in this world, it feemeth moft abfurde, that herefie doth dominiere ouer Catholike religion; which God fometimes, and in fome places fuffereth, for the greater merite of his elect.

How much more anie man profiteth in Wifdom, fo much more he is angrie with himfelf, for the euils which he hath done: fo much better he knoweth the ftrict iudgement of God, which he muft paffe: fo much more he vnderftandeth the want of perfect wifdom: fo much more he feeteth that labour is required to proceede in vertue: and to conferue that fmal portion which he hath gotten.

It is better that two be together.) Befides the commendation of charitie, and freindship amongft men, S. Ierom expoundeth it alfo of the neceffitie of Chrifts dwelling in mans foule, & of his continual affifting grace: that man alone lie not open to the deicepts of the aduerfarie. S. Ierom teacheth, that neither can man without Chrift refift tentations, nor rife from anie finne in this life, or in the next: neither wil Chrift without mans confent and endeauour, ftay him from falling, nor raife him vp being fallen.
The 2. part. True felicitie is procured by good life, and confifteth in the eternal fight of God.
Anger rightly vfed, is commendable & beneficial to correct our owne faults and others.
But haftie, or immoderate anger is finful and hurtful.
Likewife moderate riches profite the feruants of God, fo that they fette not their mind vpon them.
Mans reafon which is the fuperiour part of his foule, rarely thinketh good.
God made man right.

As probable coniecture of a mans inward difpofition is made by his exteriour countenance: fo his good workes fhining before men are good figne of internal vertues which indeed are right and meritorious, when the intention is fincere, referring al to Gods glorie & edification of others, without defire of vaine praife in the world.
By this terme (vanitie) is ftil vnderftood that felicitie is not in profperous things of this world: neither are al men miferable, that fuffer aduerfitie.

Mortal men fuffering calamities know not whether the fame be inflicted for their proofe and merite, as in Iob and Tobie, or for their finnes only, as in Pharao, & the Aegyptians: but fhal know in the iudgement after their death.
A finner in this life may amend if he wil, & become iuft: but after death he can not repent.
Myftically, the Gentiles were iudged by Salomon better then the Iewes. S. Ierom.
Bafe & vicious men mixt with the good corrupt the whole companie: much more a mortal finne in a mans foule deftroyeth al the vertues, that were there before.
Euil men aduanced feeme to profper.
But they fal into their owne traps.
In Kings and al fuperiours are required mature age, & diligent care of the common good. Antiquitie in matter of faith and religion is to be followed, not noueltie.
Of al vertues, the workes of mercie, corporal and fpiritual, moft auaile for obtaining eternal felicitie. Mat. 25.
So the fame be grounded in true faith, belieuing al that is written in the old and new teftament, fignified by feuen & eight.
After death, none can either merite or demerite.
Both in youth and old age doe good workes.

An admonition to al in general to liue wel in this world, remembring the day of general iudgement, before which fuch fignes fhal come as are defcribed here, and by our Sauiour Mat. 24. And likewife euerie one is admonifhed in particular to ferue God diligently whiles he hath time, before death come, when al his fenfes & former helpes fhal faile.
This is the briefe fumme of al profitable doctrine: Feare God, & keepe his commandments.
The Church of the old testament desireth Christs comming in flesh: and the Christian Church prayeth for his comming in glorie.
The Church outwardly afflicted, is inwardly fayre.
Christ encourageth his spouse the Church.
She meditateth of his Passion and Resurrection.
Christ praiseth his spouse.
She againe praiseth him.
With thankes for her repose, and present consolation.
Christ professeth himself the flowre of mankind: yea Lord of al creatures.
The Church excelleth al other societies: In the Church the godlie excel sinners: Among the innocent and holie, the Virgin Marie surpasseth al.
He for the weakes sake permitteth her not to be molested, til she be prepared to suffer with patience.
The Church praising Christ vnder his protection.
She feeling Christs assistance, confesseth, & preacheth boldly his Ghospel & truth against al Pagans and Heretikes.
Yet encourageth her to approch vnto him.
And so she reposeth in him.
Christ againe praiseth the beautie of his Church. Sincere and simple intention.
Pastours who like nurces giue bread of good doctrine to litle ones.
Faith and good workes.
Preaching Christs passion.
And not ashamed to professe Christ crucified.
Administration of Sacraments whereby the Church, Christs mystical bodie, is ioyned to him her head.
Which is an inexpugnable fortresse.
Both Iewes and Gentils are fed with the principles of Christian doctrine.
Christ dwelleth in mortified, and deuout minds. The Church triumphant is without spot, and euerie particular soule entring into heauen: the B. Virgin Mother was also in this life alwayes Immaculate.
The spouse condescending to Gods wil, is wel content to suffer persecution.
Christ againe sheweth his good liking in his spouses patience.
The spouse desireth to rest in meditation.
And vrged by Christs owne example working for al mankind.
Conferring together, the describing the excellencies of Christ.
And resolue to seeke him, wheresoever he be.
The Church teacheth her children that Christ is delighted with the godlie desires, and fruitful workes of the faithful.
Christ againe commendeth his Church, wel composed of distinct orders, al together making a complete armie, terrible to al enimies.
The more anie contemplate Gods Maiestie, the better they perceiue that he is incomprehensible.
Al which are but one bodie in vnitie of faith.
The voice of the old Synagogue, admiring the beautie of Christs Church.
The Church of Christ exhorteth the Synagogue of the Iewes to returne to Christ.
Christ interposeth his commendation of the Iewes, who at last shal returne to him with great feruour of faith and deuotion.
And so iointly praiseth his Church consisting of both peoples.
The Church as it were taking the word out of Christs mouth whiles he praised her, she returned al the praise to him.
Praying him to come and stil remaine with her.
Acknowledging him to be the only Sauiour of both old and new testament.
The Synagogue prosecuteth her prayer, desiring Christs Incarnation.
Christ admonisheth worldlie men not to molest those that serue him: in contemplation, & other spiritual vertues.

Angels and other Saints of the triumphant Church admire the beautie of the Gentiles conuerted: which is also vnderstood of euerie holie soule ascending from this world into heauen: And more singularly of the most glorious Virgin Mother of God.
The Synagogue of the Iewes was corrupt vnder the tree of Christs Crosse, when they cried: Crucifie him. And againe: His bloud be vpon vs, and vpon our children.
Christ againe sheweth his affection towards his Church of the Gentiles: calling her his owne sister, promising and bestowing on her manie excellent benefits.
The Church of Gentiles reioyceth in the strong defence, wherwith her Sauiour hath established her.
Keepers of this vineyard were the Prophets and Apostles, and their Successors are stil the keepers therof.

The whole Church militant wel contented, yea desiring Christs Ascension into heauen, for the good of al that here serue him, prayeth him, from thence to send aboundance of his grace, that we may ascend the high mountaines of perfect charitie and zele of Gods honour.
Mortal sinnes are not only committed in deeds & wordes, but also in thoughtes.
He that maliciously curseth is cursed of God.
Be not cause of your owne eternall death by euil life.
Desperate death deliuereth not the wicked from calamities.
Neither are damned whiles they are in this life: But sinners not repenting bring eternall death to themselues.
Carnal men thinking the soule to be mortall, and so neither paine nor reward to be after death, fal to these prophane thoughts and practises of infidelitie.
Of infidelitie touching paine or reward after death, proceedeth the Epicures life.
An euident prophecie of the Iewes malice persecuting our Sauiour. Fulfilled by the chief Priestes, Scribes, and Ancients.
Want of belieuing diuine Mysteries, namely the reward of the iust, & punishment of the wicked, is cause of dissolute life, and of hatred against the good.
Temporal death of the iust, is the way to eternall life. Where damnation (called here the torment of death) shal not touch them.
For albeit Martyrs seeme in the eyes of the vnwise to dye, or to be extinguished, they passe into eternall, and vnspeakeable glorie.
Al the iust shal approue Gods iudgement condemning the wicked.

Chastitie of the bodie is a singular great vertue, & spirituall chastitie of true faith and religion is greater, and more generally commended, as the roote and foundation of al vertues. For without faith it is vnpossible to please God.
When fo euer the iust dieth it is profitable for him: & dying yong his immaculate life is more commendable then old age in the wicked.

He vvas taken avvay.) By this place S. Augustin proueth that such as died in good state, might haue fallen into wickednes, if they had liued longer: and therefore it was a benefit to them to dye fooner. Which allured foreknowledge of al things that shal be, or may be, standeth wel with mans free-wil.
Wicked men in their false conceipt iudge the trauels of the iust to be vaine & fruities.
Repentance of the damned is only for the losse & paine whereinto they are fallen, not of loue towards God.
Such things they in hel, which sinned.
As the ioy of the blessed, so contrariwise the miserie of the damned is maruelous great, & for euer vnchangeable.
Al power is from God: & therfore to be respected, though the magistrates sometimes abuse their authoritie.
As euerie ones charge is more or lesse, to his account is easier or harder, and the punishment if he offend smaller or greater. S. Gregorie.
Wisdom is attained by this gradation: from the most true desire of discipline, and so from first to last, a resolute desire ioyneth faithful soules to God.
Children in the mothers wombe are as in sleepe.
Salomon wished, and vnderstanding was giuen him: and he inuocated, and the spirit of wisdom came vpon him.
God first gaue him grace to desire wisdom before al other things.
Salomon was a most excellent Philosopher.
Proper Epithetons of the spirit of wisdom.
God, the increated wisdom is infinite, and wisdom created is also most excellent amongst Gods guifts.
This is also the speach of Salomon, recited by the writer of this booke.
Of Salomons wisdom, riches, glorie, & renoumed fame, not only the bookes of Kings & Paralipomenon, but also our Sauiour doth witnes. Mat. 6 & 12.
It is not certaine that Salomon hath immortal glorie, but rather by immortalitie is here vnderstood that his glorious fame remaineth to the end of this world.
It is certaine that Salomon was sometime innocent and holie, but was peruerted by women. 3. Reg. 11.
And if one be perfect among the children of men, and thy wisdom be absent from him, he shall be counted for nothing.
Our first parent Adam truly repented, and had remission of his sinne.
About Sodom there is only shew of fruit, which when it is touched faleth into dust.
When the Israelites wanted water God gave them abundance out of rockes. But turned the Aegyptians waters into bloud.
Aegyptians serving beasts for Gods, were plagued by frogges, siniphes, flyes, and locusts.
God made no creature evil, as the Manichees foolishly imagined, neither is there any God but one, who alone created al things.
From the land of Iurie, called sacred, because God was there rightly served in the old testament, and mans redemption was wrought there by Christ.
By custome malice became as it were natural, after that nature was corrupted.
Gods power being Almightie is only limited by his wil.
Knowing him to be the only true God, by whom they saw their false Gods destroyed, yet they did not serve him as God.

Gods most proper name is HE WHICH IS. Exo. 3. v. 14. Philosophers discussing the nature of manie creatures, saw that euerie creature proceeded of some other thing, & so there must needs be one beginning of al, absolute of it-selfe, neither proceeding nor depending of an other. And this is God.
Seeing no creature how excellent soever, is or may be esteemed a God, it is more foolish to thinke an Image, or statue, or anie thing framed by mans hands can be God.
Great madnes to invocate a woodden idol, more base & commonly more corruptible then the wood of a shippe.
The author prophetically alludeth to the wood of the Crosse, on which our Saviour redeemed mankind. From whose death proceedeth mans iustification.
Invention of Idols brought spiritual fornication, & corruption of manners.
The father made vnto himself the image of his sonne. Caluin here falsly chargeth this booke with errour.
The name God in the proper signification, can not be given to anie creature. Manie enormious crimes proceede from idolatrie.
Two sorts of periurie: swearing by false Gods, and swearing vntruths.
Of the diuers sorts of idols and idolaters see our briefe Annotation vpon the 113. Psal.
Idolaters having forsaken and forgot the onlie true God, become as Atheists, making their temporal gaine of false Gods.
Some idolaters worshipped brute beasts for Gods, as being better then sensles images: but al are abominable.
The Aegyptians were plagued for their idolatrie. And that by beasts, because they worshipped beasts for Gods.
The brasen serpent not by anie vertue inherent, but as a signe of Gods favour, was the meanes of curing the people.
Haile did not extinguish the fire, by Gods power above nature.
Literally the Aegyptians had darknes three dayes together. Exo. 10. v. 23.
Morally they & other gentils were in darknes without faith in God, til Christs Resurrection the third day.
A troubled conscience is a great torment.
This signified the conversion of al nations to Christ.
The Church is called holie, because it professeth holines, and hath alwayes some holie men, & without the Church there is no sanctitie.
When the Aegyptians drowned the Hebrewes children, Moyses was saved, and referred to guide the Israelites, when the Aegyptians were drowned.
A prophecie of Christ, comming into this world, when there was temporal peace, but extreme darknes of ignorance.
An other example of difference in Gods punishing his people for their amendment, and of the obstinate vnto their ruine.
God foreseeing the Aegyptians malice, permitted them to persecute his people, but was no way the cause nor authour of their sinne.
The Amorhites refused to grant them passage. The Aegyptians brought them into servitude.
God changing the naturall properties of elements, wrought iustice on sinners.
Constancie in good purpose meriteth the promised peace, and iust reward.
Manie preferre learning before pietie, which S. Augustin reproueth, preferring the godlie before the learned that are lesse vertuous.
Whatfoeuer anie foweth that he shal reape, and the wicked shal eate the fruits of his owne workes.
Great prudence and fortitude are required in al Iudges spiritual and temporal.
In hel are diuers punishments, fire, burning, and the worme of conscience, vexing the soule, & both are eternall.
Workes of mercie are also profitable to the dead, as prayer, almes, and sacrifice for soules in purgatorie.
A most especiall preseruatiue from sinne.
Briberie sometimes corrupteth Kings, much more other inferiour Iudges. And therfore it is better to suffer damage then to contend by law against the rich.
In al consultations conferre with the skilfull; for the blinde can not iudge of colours, the sicke of taste: nor worldlie men of spirituall things.
Seeing iealosie between man & wife is dangerous, much more vnnecessarie conuersation betweene other men and women: especially probable occasion of sinne is to be auoided.
Constancie in good things, namely in freindship, is very necessarie.
Example of rulers is of great efficacie.
The causes of translating Kingdomes, & dominions.
Couetousnes is the roote of al euils. 1.Tim. 6. In that for lucre manie fall into al fortes of sinnes, euen into schisme and heresie.
Neuerthelesse pride is the beginning of al sinne, as this text expresly testifieth: and the reason is, for that mans inordinate selfe-loue is the cause of declining from Gods commandments.
A wiseman humbling himselfe by penance, as Daniel did, or being vniustly humbled by others, as Ioseph was, shal be exalted by God.
Expect the end of an other mans speach, before you begin to answer.
One punishment of sinne is blindnes of hart, especially where is no remorse of conscience.
In prosperitie is feare, and in aduersitie hope of change.
It is rather crueltie then mercie to nourish a wicked man persisting in sinne: for so he runneth stil into more wickednes, and into eternall damnation.
But the penitent is to be comforted and assisted.
Euerie one is bound to loue his enemie of charitie; but in prudence it behoueth not to credite him. According to our Sauiours rule: Be wise as serpents and simple as doues. Mat. 10.
He that toucheth pich, shal be defiled with it: and he that communicateth with the proud, shal put on pride.
Be not humble in thy wifdom, left humbled thou be seduced into pusillanimicie.
Say not: It is by God, that she is absent: for doe not thou the things that he hateth. God iustly decreed that which men vniustly haue done.
God from the beginning made man, and left him in the hand of his owne counsel.
He hath commanded no man to doe impiously, and he hath giuen no man space to sinne: for he desireth not a multitude of faithlesse and vnprofitable children.
For better is one fearing God, then a thousand impious children.
Al mercie shal make a place to euerie man according to the merite of his workes, & according to the vnderstanding of his peregrination.
Be not hindred to pray alwayes, & feare not to be iustified euen to death, because the reward of God abideth for euer.
As a serpent deceitfully approcheth & stingeth the bodie, so al sinnes inuegle and hurt the soule.
Wicked men condemning the diuel or anie other wicked, doe indeed condemne themselues. And to them agreeth that sentence of our Sauiour: By thyne owne mouth I iudge thee, naughtie servant. Luc. 19.
Contempt & ignominie is the worldlie punishment of the slouthful, besides his eternal damnation at the day of iudgement. Mat. 25. v. 30.
Man being weake and the enemie surtie & strong, he is not able to resist tentations without Gods special grace, for which the wiseman therefore prayeth.
Against rash, vntrue, & vnlawful swearing.
In oathes God is called to witnes as he that can not lie, but blasphemie attributeth that to God which perteyneth not to him, and so is a contrarie sinne to vnlawful swearing. Both are mortal sinnes.
Seeing carnal adulterie is seuerly punished, much more spiritual: as heresie, and apostasie from Catholike Religion.
Diuine wisedome, the Second Person of the B. Trinitie, begotten, not created, praiseth it self.
God offereth his grace, but forceth not anie to accept it.
Creation is not here taken in the strict signification, but for diuine production, in that God the Father by vnderstanding begetteth God the Sonne.
The more grace any hath, the more he desireth and receiueth.
Al happines in this life is grounded in the feare of God.
The description of heresie vnder the figure of a wicked woman: whose malice is secretly couered vnder pretence of truth and pietie.
Lay-headship in spiritual causes is vnreasonable and absurde.
An vnquiet life depriueth a man of much comfort: and therfore continual peace is as double life.
As when oxen moue, the yoke on their necks doth moue withal: so a wicked woman (to wit heresie) can not rest, nor let others rest quiet.
The Catholike faith is the ground of al vertues.
The soule is kept in good state by fearing God.
After that sinne is purged there remaines relikes in the soule, as dust in a seue when the chaffe is cast out, til it be more purged or washed.
Whether the sunne shineth forth or not, it is alwayes light: so is a wise man always vertuous.
A foole, or wicked man, hath no light of vertue in himselfe (like the moone) but sometimes seemeth to haue more light, sometimes lesse, sometimes none at al. S. Bernard.
He that exceedingly doth iniurie to an other is justly punished also in this world.
He that seeketh reuenge contrarie to the course of iustice, or of euil intention or of rancor of mind, sinneth grieuously.
Lending is a worke of mercie, & a kinde of almes. To repay that is borowed is a worke of iustice: witingly not to restore is as bad as theft.
Prudence requireth that by helping an other, thou doest not overthrow thy self.
The eldest sonne being heyre to his father, is to haue special care of the other children, euen to the aduenturing of his owne hurt for their liues.
Pietie is aboue al riches and honour.
Suffer not vnnecessarie pensiuenes to afflict thy mind, through pusillanimirie. But relie wholly vpon Gods wil and prouidence, resigning thy wil vnto his.
They that imploy al their studie to get vertues, shal be more free from tentations of the flesh.
It is in mans freewil to transgresse, & therefore they are happie, that through Gods grace doe not breake his commandments.
As bread is the chiefe sustenance of the bodie, so doctrine is of the minde, which being good nourisheth, & if it be bad corrupteth them that receiue it.
Humilitie is necessarie in al, but most especially in men of auctoritie. The greatest art in this life is to contemne vaine glorie in height of auctoritie. S. Greg. de cura pastoralis.
God directeth mens resolutions, and actions, when they vse the meanes of consultation, as he hath appointed.
Men led with passions runne from one vice into an other without ceasing. Especially heretikes runne into manie errours.
Vaine dreames are not to be regarded: but some are good & from God. Whatsoever is written in holy Scripture is vndoubtedly true.
God also prouideth that alwayes there be some which truly explicate his law.
He that destroyeth that an other buildeth, bringeth two mens labours to nothing. Recidiuation into sinne maketh the former repentance frustrate.
Sacrifices of penance, of iustice, and of praise, are better then external sacrifices.
Neuerthelesse external sacrifice is also necessarie.
This prayer imputeth also a prophecie of the conuersion of the Gentils.
As freindship is a most necessarie thing in humane life; so feined freindship is most dangerous.
Man is bound to vse al prudent endeavour, & withal most especially pray for Gods direction.
The dayes of the Blessed in heaven, who see God, are eternal.
Mans bodie naturally needeth sustenance, & sometimes phisike, and so doth his soule; which as it is more excellent, so it ought to be more regarded.
Spiritual infirmitie requireth spiritual phisike.
To auoide euil speach, and shew that thou art truly sorie for the death of thy freind. But moderate thy sorow, lest it hurt thy selfe.
When pastours haue leisure from preaching, they may then profite others by writing. So S. Paul not only preached, but also writte.
Besides actiue life commended in the former chap. it is necessarie that vertuous men vse also meditation & contemplation.
True knowledge of holie Mysteries: without which meditation wil be erronious.
Puritie of soule free from grieuous sinnes, and endowed with vertues.
Humilitie is especially required in contemplatiue persons.
Hope of eternal reward comforteth, & encourageth the seruants of God.
In the meane time God giueth necessaries for this life. Which the good vse rightly to their merite, & the wicked vse euil to their damnation.
Diuels were created in state of grace, & of their owne wil fel from God, & consequently were punished.
The penalties which al men euen new borne infants suffer, doe shew that al in general are guiltie of original sinne.
The societie of Christs Church flourishing in al vertues excelleth the benefits of the old Testament.
Euerie one is bound to labour that he want not necessaries.
It is to no purpose after death to accuse the shortnes or length of life; pretending the same to haue been cause of sinne.
It behoueth children, and teachers to esteeme that which their elders teach: though the same doth not seeme reasonable in their owne opinion.
It is not lawful to repeate that which we iustly promise to conceale.
There is more danger in conuersing familiarly with a wicked man, then with a freindlie woman, in which conversation much prudence is required.
The excellencie of God, which can not be seen with mortal eye, is proposed to our meditation in his workes.
Of al sensible creatures the sunne is most excellent. Of which al corporal creatures receiue their light: & by whose influence al generation of creatures proceedeth.
Vertuous men are rightly called Lords, and Princes.
Enoch shal preach penaunce in the time of Antichrist.
Noe was perfect.
Abraham father of al the beliefers in Christ.
Isaac and Iacob were blessed in Abraham.
Moyses saw Gods workes more cleerly then other Prophets, yet saw not his substance, as is noted Exod. 33.
Aarons Priesthood continued so long as Moyses law: that was til Christ. And now the Priesthood according to the order of Melchisedech continueth to the end of the world.
The tribe of Leui had not a portion of inheritance separate from the rest, but had tithes, first fruits, and oblations for their temporal prouision.
Iosue succeeded in the temporal gouernement: for the spiritual to the successours of Aaron.
Only Iosue and Caleb remained of those which came out of Aegypt, al the rest dyed in the desert; and their children entered into Chanaan.
Though some of the Iudges were sometimes great sinners, yet they were finally iust, & for their good actes much renowned.
Samuel annointed Saul and Dauid Kings.
If Samuel himself had not appeared, (but some other spirite) it could not haue been noted in his praises. See 1. Reg. 28.
Among al the renowned actes of Dauid his pure and sincere hart most pleased God.
For Dauids sake God gaue wisdome to Salomon, and peace in his kingdom.
Salomons sinnes were punished, but Gods mercie continued in conferuing his posteritie. Psal. 88.
Elias procured fire from heauen to burne his sacrifice, 3. Reg. 18. and twice more to burne an hundred men which persecuted him. 4. Reg. 1.
Not only this booke but other holie Scriptures also witnes that Elias shal returne and preach before the end of the world. Mal. 4. Mat. 17.
The miracle wrought by his dead bodie shewed that he was an holie Prophet.
Prayer preuailed, when forces were not sufficient.
Manie other Kings of Iuda restrained alwaies from committing idolatrie, but these three destroyed al places of idolatrie in their kingdom.
Ioseph prophecying that the people should depart from Aegypt, willed them to carie his bones with them.
This Simon called Iustus, and Priscus, was high Priest when this booke was written, a very holie man, dead before it was translated into Greeke about 300 yeares before Christ.
Three nations; the Idumeans, Philistimes, and Samaritans, did most persecute the Israelites.
They are also called a foolish people, because they knowing true religion mixed idolatrie therwith.
Where we are not able to render recompence to benefactours, especially to God, we are the more bound to acknowledge his manie great benefits, altogether vndeserued by vs.

A way which seemeth just.) If any Jewes, Turkes, or Heretikes lead a moral good life in this world, it seemeth both to themselves, and to other rude people, that they are in a right way of salvation, but their errour in faith leadeth them to eternal damnation.

Health of the hart.) As foundnes of the hart conserveth the rest of the same bodie in life, so a pure intention often excuseth from mortal sinne, as in errours committed of probable, not of grosse, nor affected ignorance. But secret envie in the hart infecteth and putrifieth mans actions, and destroyeth the worke that seemed good: which can no more endure strict examination in the day of judgement, then a rotten cloth can abide washing.

A valiant woman who shal finde?) Upon occasion of his mothers most prudent admonition, the wiseman singularly praiseth a perfect vertuous woman. And that in an exquisite kind of stile: in Tetrametricall Iambike verse, with perfect order and number of the Alphabet letters, signifying, as S. Ierom teacheth, that as none can reade, or speake words, vnles they first learne to know the letters: so we can not attaine to know the greater Mysteries in holie Scriptures, except we beginne with moral good life, according to that the Prophet sayth: By thy commandments I have understood. And therefore wise Salomon, by instinct of the Holie Ghost, as wel by the manner of stile, as by the doctrine conteined, concludeth his Booke of Parables, with praise both of the Church in general, which hath al the vertues, and good properties here mentioned, and of everie faithful soule, sincerely serving God, which either in deed, or in desire of mind, hath such part of them, as may suffice to the attaining of eternal life.
The Church of the old testament desireth Christs comming in flesh: and the Christian Church prayeth for his comming in glorie.
The Church outwardly afflicted, is inwardly fayre.
Christ encourageth his spouse the Church.
She meditateth of his Passion and Resurrection.
Christ praiseth his spouse.
She againe praiseth him.
With thankes for her repose, and present consolation.
Christ professeth himselfe the flowre of mankind: yea Lord of al creatures.
The Church excelleth al other societies: In the Church the godlie excel sinners: Among the innocent and holie, the Virgin Marie surpasseth al.
He for the weakes sake permitteth her not to be molested, til she be prepared to suffer with patience.
The Church praising Christ reciteth secure vnder his protection.

She feeling Christs assistance, confesseth, and preacheth boldly his Gospel and truth against al Pagans and Heretikes. Who though he shew not himselfe visibly, yet encourageth her to approch vnto him: commandeth his pastours to destroy heresies. And so she reposeth in him.
The Church finding Christ not in darke ignorance nor in philosophie, but by his reueiling himselfe to her, holdeth him for euer: even til the Iewes shal at last also find him.
The Church of Christ admireth her owne conversion from Gentilitie, now ful of good Workes.

She also professeth that the ascending to eternal rest, is by fighting manfully, in observing the ten commandements, in the six dayes of this life: even to bloud, if neede be; which is the highest degree of charitie. And inuiteth al other to come vnto Christ, who in the flesh which he tooke of his mother, was crowned in heauen after his passion.
Christ againe praiseth the beautie of his Church. Sincere and simple intention. Al her temporal occupations directed to Gods glorie.
Pastours who like nurces give bread of good doctrine to litle ones.
Faith and good workes.
Preaching Christs passion.
And not ashamed to professe Christ crucified.
Administration of Sacraments whereby the Church, Christs mystical bodie, is ioyned to him her head, which is an inexpugnable fortresse.

Christ dwelleth in mortified, and devout minds. The Church triumphant is without spot, and everie particular soule entring into heauen; the B. Virgin Mother was also in this life alwayes Immaculate. Al tentations, whether they be in manifest crueltie, or in flatering subteltie, make constant soules more gratefull to God.
Both Iewes and Gentils are fed with the principles of Christian doctrine.

The spouse condescending to Gods wil, is wel content to suffer perfection. Christ againe sheweth his good liking in his spouses patience: and willeth the glorious Saints to congratulate with the patient.
The spouse desireth to rest in meditation: and is urged by Christs owne example working for al mankind.
As the joy of the blessed, so contrariwise the miserie of the damned is marvelous great, and for ever unchangeable.
The Church teacheth her children that Christ is delighted with the lilies, and godlie desires, and fruitful workes of the faithful.
Christ againe commendeth his Church.

The more anie contemplate Gods Maiestie, the better they perceiue that he is incomprehensible. Manie true pastours, more hyrelings, that also preach truth, but for temporal commoditie. And innumerable faithful soules in the Church. Al which are but one bodie in vnitie of faith.
The voice of the old Synagogue, admiring the beautie of Christs Church.
The Church of Christ exhorteth the Synagogue of the Iewes to returne to Christ.
Christ interposeth his commendation of the Iewes, who at last shal returne to him with great feruour of faith and devotion.
And so iointly praiseth his Church consisting of both peoples.
The Church as it were taking the word out of Christs mouth whiles he praised her, she returned al the praise to him.
Praying him to come and fille her with himselfe.
Acknowledging him to be the only Sauiour of both old and new testament.
The Synagogue professeth her prayer, desiring Christs Incarnation.
Christ admonisheth worldlie men not to molest those that serue him; in contemplation, and other spiritual vertues.

Angels and other Saints of the triumphant Church admire the beautie of the Gentiles converted: which is also understood of everie holie soule ascending from this world into heauen: And more singularly of the most glorious Virgin Mother of God.

The Synagogue of the Iewes was corrupt vnder the tree of Christs Crosse, when they cried: Crucifie him, Crucifie him. Christ againe sheweth his affection towards his Church of the Gentiles: calling her his owne sister, and the Synagogues sister, promising and bestowing on her manie excellent benefits. The Church of Gentiles reioyceth in the strong defence, wherwith her Sauiour hath established her. Christ sheweth that together with the pastors, himself especially hath care of his Church, alwayes assisting the visible gouernors therof with his inuisible grace. The whole Church militant wel contented, yea desiring Christs Ascension into heauen, for the good of al that here serue him, prayeth him, from thence to send aboundance of his grace.
Mortal sinnes are not only committed in deeds and wordes, but also in thoughts.
He that maliciously curseth is cursed of God.
Be not cause of your owne eternall death.
Desperate death delivereth not the wicked from calamities.
Neither are damned whiles they are in this life.
But sinners not repenting bring eternall death to themselves.
Carnal men thinking the soule to be mortall doe commit al manner of sinnes.
Of infidelity touching paine or reward after death proceede al disorders in life.
An evident prophecie of the Iewes malice persecuting our Saviour, fulfilled by the chief Priestes, Scribes, and Ancients.
Want of belieuing diuine Mysteries.
Temporal death of the iust is the way to eternall life.
For albeit Martyrs seeme in the eyes of the vnwise to dye or to be extinguished, they passe into eternall, and vnspeakeable glorie.
Literall sinne is cause of dissolute life, and of hatred against the good.
Chastitie of the bodie is a singular great vertue, and spiritual chastitie of true faith and religion is greater.
When the iust dieth it is profitable for him: and dying yong his immaculate life is more commendable then old age in the wicked.

He was taken away.) By this place S. Augustin proueth that such as died in good state, might haue fallen into wickednes, if they had liued longer: and therefore it was a benefit to them to dye sooner. And that it is neuertheles certaine; that God both knew the possibilitie that such might, yea would haue sinned, if they had liued longer, and also knew that they should die sooner, and so escape that danger. Which assured foreknowledge of al things that shal be, or may be, standeth wel with mans free-wil: against certaine that inclined to Pelagianisme, attributing too much to mans free-wil, and detracting from Gods foreknowledge and prouidence. li. 1. de Predest. Sanct. ch. 14.

Wicked men in their false conceit iudge the trauels of the iust to be vaine and fruitles. Repentance of the damned is only for the losse and paine whereinto they are fallen, not of loue towards God. The damned shal be without excuse, vtterly confounded in their owne consciences.
As the joy of the blessed, so contrariwise the miserie of the damned is marvelous great, and for ever unchangeable.
Al power is from God: and therfore to be respected, though the magistrates sometimes abuse their authoritie.
As everie ones charge is more or lesse, so his account is easier or harder, and the punishment (if he offend) finallie or greater.
Wisdom is attained by this gradation: from first to last, a resolute desire ioyneth faithful soules to God.
Salomon praised for wisdom, and obtained it by prayer.
God first gaue him grace to desire wisdom before al other things.
Salomon was a most excellent Philosopher.
Proper Epithetons of the spirit of wisdom.
See the Annotation Prouerb 1. v. 2. (Wisdom is a vapour of the power of God, and a certaine sincere emanation of the glorie of God omnipotent.)
God, the increated wisdom is infinite, and wisdom created is also most excellent amongst Gods guifts.
Of Salomons wisdom, riches, glorie, and renowned fame, not only the bookes of Kings and Paralipomenon, but also our Saviour doth witnes.
It is not certaine that Salomon hath immortal glorie, but rather by immortalitie is here understood that his glorious fame remaineth to the end of this world.
Neuertheles wisdom of her part giueth life and glorie euerlasting, to al that perseuer to the end of this life.
It is certaine that Salomon was sometime innocent and holie, but was peruerted by women.
Here againe it is euident, that the Author reporteth Salomons speaches.
Wisdom increated is with God, yea is God himselfe.
Wisdom which is giuen to men proceedeth from God.
Mans wisdom without special wisdom from God is not sufficient to gouerne our selues, much lesse others.
Adam. By this it is certaine that our first parent Adam truly repented, and had remission of his sinne.
Noe.
Abraham. Lot. In al trees about Sodom there is only shew of fruit, which when it is touched falleth into dust.
Lots wife: an example of inconstancie.
Iacob.
Ioseph.
Gods peculiar people.
The Amalachites.
When the Israelites wanted water God gaue them abundance out of rockes. But turned the Aegyptians waters into bloud.
After affliction the benefit of peace is more grateful. Moyses was reiected when he iudged betweene his brethren, but was afterwards the deliverer of the whole people.
Aegyptians serving beasts for Gods, were plagued by frogges, siniphes, flies, and locusts.
God made no creature euil, as the Manichees foolishly imagined, neither is there any God but one, who alone created al things.
From the land of Iurie, called sacred, because God was there rightly served in the old testament, and mans redemption was wrought there by Christ.
By custome malice became as it were natural, after that nature was corrupted.
Gods power being Almightie is only limited by his wil.
Serpents, hares, moles, and like beastes, which seeme not only superfluous in the world, but also hurtful, yet were esteemed as Gods.
Knowing him to be the only true God, by whom they saw their false Gods destroyed, yet they did not serve him as God.

They could not understand him.) Philosophers discussing the nature of manie creatures, saw that euerie creature proceeded of some other thing, and so there must needs be one beginning of al, absolute of it-selfe, neither proceeding nor depending of an other, nor a limited substance. Whom some Philosophers, by such discourse found, and knew in general, and sometimes confessed, but did not honour him as God, and therefore were inexcusable, as S. Paule concludeth against them.
Great madnes to invocate a woodden idol more base and commonly more corruptible then the wood of a shippe. As the Israelites went through the red sea.
The author prophetically alludeth to the wood of the Crosse, on which our Saviour redeemed mankind.
From whose death proceedeth mans iustification.
Invention of Idols brought spiritual fornication, and corruption of manners.

The father made vnto himself the image of his sonne.) Caluin here chargeth this booke with errour, in affirming that idolatrie began by superficiously honouring images of the dead. Against which he alleadgeth that Labans idols, and others more ancient, were before anie images of dead men were honoured. But he argueth vpon a false ground. For Labans idols were images, as the Hebrew word Theraphim signifieth. It is also certaine that Ninus King of the Assyrians, long before Laban, yea before Abraham, set vp the image of his Father Belus to be publikly honoured by the people, as S. Cyril sheweth. Likewise S. Cyprian, S. Chrysostom, and Egesippus testifie, that the making of mens images in memorie of the dead, was the occasion and beginning of idolatrie.
Of the divers sorts of idols and idolaters. Idolaters having forsaken and forgot the only true God, become slaves to shameful passions.
Some idolaters worshipped brute beasts for Gods, as being senseless images: but all are abominable.

The Aegyptians were plagued for their idolatry. And that by beasts, because they worshipped beasts for Gods: and by death of their first-begotten, for their cruelty against Gods people. God punished his owne people as a father, for their amendment.
The brazen serpent not by any virtue inherent, but as a sign of Gods favour, was the means of curing the people.
With the plague of hail there was also fire mixed, which destroyed the profitable cattle.
But burnt not other beasts, that plagued the Aegyptians. Hail did not extinguish the fire, by Gods power above nature.
See the miracles of Manna, Annot. Exod. 16.
Literally the Aegyptians had darkness three dayes together. Morally they and other gentiles were in darkness without faith in God, til Christs Resurrection the third day.
A troubled conscience is always presuming cruel things.
This signified the conversion of all nations to Christ.
The Church is called holie, because it professeth holines, and hath alwayes some holie men, and without the Church there is no sanctitie.
When the Aegyptians drowned the Hebrewes children, Moyses was saved, and appointed to guide the Israelites, when the Aegyptians were drowned.
A prophecie of Christ, coming when things being incredible because of the inchantments, then first when there was destruction of the first-begotten, they promised to be the people of God.
An other example of difference in Gods punishing his people for their amendment, and of the obstinate vnto their ruine.
God foreseeing the Aegyptians malice, permitted them to persecute his people, but was no way the cause nor authour of their sinne.
The Amorhites refused to grant them passage. The Aegyptians brought them into servitude.
God changing the natural properties of elements, by that wrought iustice on sinners.
Mans wisdom is not able to comprehend the works of God.

Eternal glory is the fruit of the fear of our Lord: this one virtue fulfilleth al, but it is the beginning, grounded in true faith, and bringeth forth other vertues, divine gifts, with the fruits of the Holy Ghost, and a ioyful crowne in the end.
Men drowned in sinne thinke the service of God a most tedious and loathsome thing.
Desire not to dye that thou maist thereby be covered from worldlie miseries, for that is a desperate desire, but haue patience.
As vertues are to be rewarded: so al sinnes shal be punished.
Constancie in good purpose meriteth the promised peace, and iust reward.

The progenie of Gods children bring forth the fruit of obedience and loue. Honour and estimation of parents is the chiefest obligation that man hath towards his neighbour, and the first after his dutie to God.
Almes given, or prayer made, or sacrifice offered for parents, doth merite reward at Gods hand.
Al greatnes in men proceeding from God, bindeth them so much the more to humilitie, which vertue God most specially loueth: that so they may shew gratitude for his benefites.
Those that dare liue in sinful state, tempting God euen to the last houre, commonly perish therein.
Give not occasion by thy hard dealing with the poore, that they curse thee; for if thou give cause, God who is their protectour, wil revenge them against thee.
They that follow wisdom shal be safe.
Through shamefastnes to yeald vnto sinne, or not to reproue sinne, is vicious.
But to be ashamed, and to abhorre sinne is very good and necessarie.
Al men are bound to say the truth at convenient times, and euer bound to avoide vntruths. Everie one is bound rather to loose his life, then to doe against iustice, or to denie the truth.
After that sinne is forgiven there remaineth oftentimes punishment for satisfaction.

They are happy (in hope) that decline from euil. Hebrewe stile here mentioneth first the lesse euil and the greatest last: He is most happie that hath not sat, that is, hath not settled himself in wickednes, nor finally persisted obstinat: more happie, that hath not stood, not anie notable time continued in sinne: and most happie, that hath not gone, not giuen anie consent at al to euil suggestions.

Iustice consisteth in fleeing euil and doing good: the wil desiring and diligently endeuouring to walke in the way of vertue and law of God. Which is true iustice, and right forme of good life, proposed in this Psalme, for attayning eternal beatitude.

Kings and Princes against Christ. When Christ and his Apostles preached the Ghospel, both Iewes and Gentiles, with their Princes, Kings, and Emperours most furiously resisted, but al in vaine. For they could not hinder the wil and power of God. But the more they persecuted, the more was increased the zeale and number of Christians. Persecution cannot hinder the glorie of Christ.

The Gentiles thine inheritance. By this promise of God to Christ, S. Augustine conuinced the Donatists, and in them the Protestants, that say the Church of Christ is failed and become smal or inuisible: as though Christ the Sonne of God could sometimes lose his inheritance, which is the Catholike Church, gathered of the Gentiles, and his possession extended to the ends of the earth.

Thou shalt rule them in a rod of yron. If a potters vessel be broken whiles it is soft, it may easily be repayred, but after it is hard, it can not. So sinners are more easily restored to grace shortly repenting, then long obdurate. And that which is possible to man, is possible to God. For as clay in the potters hand, feare you in my hand, faith our Lord, Ier. 18. Also afterward Kings and Emperours belieued in Christ. Princes are not enemies to Christianitie: but are enemies to heretikes, the rebelles of Christ and his Church.

Pfalme of Dauid. All Interpreters agreeably teach that King Dauid made not the titles of the Psalmes. Neuerthelesse they are authentical, as indited by the Holie Ghost. The third Psalme is called the Psalme of Dauid, not because he is the onely authour thereof, for he is also authour of the former, where his name is not expressed, but because it treateth particularly and literally of him.

King Dauid by his sleeping in persecution, and his resting and deliuerie from his persecutours, prefigured Christs Death, Burial and Resurrection. As appeareth, Ioan. 2. v. 22, where the Euangelist faith that after Christs Resurrection his disciples belieued the Scripture: for otherwise the old Testament doth not so expresly declare such Mysteries as the Ghospel doth, but one thing in the proper and grammatical signification of the words, and an other thing in shadowes and figures, and both literal.

Iust anger is good and necessarie against sinne, but then is most need to beware not to exceede in passion. Haue therefore a continual purpose neuer to sinne. Euil cogitations: bewaile and repent before you sleepe.
Not only external but most especially internal sacrifice of iustice and obseruation of Gods commandements is most necessarie. The solide rewards are promised by God.
Reason and grace are freely giuen to man, whereby he may know that God wil reward the iust.

Not a God that wil iniquity: Seeing God wil not iniquity as these words testify in plaine termes, it followeth necessarily that he is not authour nor cause of anie sinne. For God doth nothing contrarie to his owne wil. But he hateth iniquity, and in respect thereof hateth al that worke iniquities, as the authours of iniquity; though he loueth them as his creatures, and of his part requireth their saluation.

By fury is signified diuine iustice, irreuocably condemning the wicked to eternall damnation: by wrath, Gods fatherly chastisement correcting sinners whom he saueth. Whereupon S. Gregorie teacheth that the faithful soule not only feareth fury, but also wrath: because after the death of the flesh some are deputed to eternal torments, some passe to life through the fire of purgation. Which doctrine the same holy Father confirmeth by the iudgement of S. Augustine more ancient, who likewise affirmeth that those which haue not laid Christ their foundation are rebuked in fury: and those which vpon right foundation haue built vpon it, wood, hay, stubble, are chastised in wrath, because they are brought to rest of beatitude, but purged by fire.

For the Octaue: Literally it seemeth that the Psalmes which haue For the Octaue in their titles, were to be sung on an instrument of eight strings. But propheticaly S. Augustine and others expound it to perteine to the Resurrection in the end of this world. So Dauid and al penitent sinners bewaile their sinnes and doe penance in this life, for the octaue, that is for the world to come.

Preffes. Most Hebrew Doctors say the word Gittith may either signifie the place where this Psalme was made, or the musicall instrument on which it was sung. But most Christian Doctors expound it literally of Christs Passion, who was stretched on the Crosse, and al his sacred bloud pressed and drawne out of his bodie. Which Metaphor Isaias also vseth, demanding of Christ: Why is thy clothing redde, and thy garments as theirs that tread in the wine preffes? Morally it signifieth the trauels of the Church militant: patience and fortitude in afflictions, whereby the good are purified.

Sela. The Septuagint, Theodotion, and Symmachus translate Diapsalma, that is, change of metre or rest in musike. Aquila, whom Ierome rather approueth, translateth into English it seemeth to most Interpreters to be added as a note to stirre vp attention. It occurreth often not onely in the end of Psalmes but also in other places. Maketh no argument that this Psalme should be diuided into two.

Vnto the end. S. Augustine applieth it to heretikes, persuading Catholikes to repaire vnto their separate congregation, falsely calling it the mountaine. Perfecutors vsing all rigour and force subtly pretend other causes against the innocent to destroy them.
